result:
The first time I heard a glacier cry, I was alone on the east shoulder of Root Nunatak in southern Alaska, 2,300 feet above the bergschrund that snaked like a badly healed scar across the flank of the icefield. It was two in the morning, the week before solstice, and the sky was the color of a bruise that refuses to turn yellow. I had gone up there to escape the smell of diesel and wet wool in the climber’s hut, carrying only a thermos of coffee gone cold and a notebook whose pages were already warped by humidity. My plan was to write something—anything—that would prove to me I was still capable of shaping the world into sentences. Instead, I listened.

The sound began as a sigh, the kind you make when you discover yet another mismatched sock at the bottom of the laundry basket, and then it deepened into a long, wet exhale, as though the planet itself were trying to whistle with a split lip. I felt it in my femurs before I heard it with my ears, a sub-audible vibration that traveled through the basalt under my boots and up into the hollow spaces behind my eyes. I thought of my mother, fifteen years dead, humming lullabies off-key while she folded my tiny T-shirts on the ironing board. I thought of the way her voice would crack on the high note of “Moon River,” how she always pretended it was the iron’s fault, how I always pretended to believe her.

I sat down on a boulder still warm from yesterday’s sun and pressed both palms against it, as if I could siphon some of that residual heat into the winter that was camped inside my chest. The glacier sounded again—crack, drip, groan—three syllables in a language older than any alphabet. I remembered reading somewhere that ice has a memory, that each air bubble trapped inside it is a time capsule of atmospheric gossip: how many sheep farted in 1126, how many jet engines screamed over the pole in 1983. I wondered what memory was being exhaled now, what antique sky was being returned to circulation. I wondered if it smelled like my mother’s Chanel No. 5, or like the formaldehyde that lingered in the hallway outside the oncology ward where I said goodbye to her shadow through a pane of glass.

The notebook stayed closed. Words felt suddenly juvenile, like trying to lasso a whale with a shoelace. So I did the only sensible thing: I talked back. Not in English, not even in words, but in the same preverbal grammar the glacier was using: a slow contraction of diaphragm, a timed release of breath, a small click at the back of my throat that my roommate in grad school once told me sounded like a Geiger counter giving up. I offered the sound as tribute, or maybe as apology—for every plastic spoon I ever threw away, for every transatlantic flight I took to “find myself,” for the fact that I had come here in part to harvest scenery for a spectacle I would later sell to an outdoor-gear company whose supply chain relied on coal plants wheezing in the hinterlands of Inner Mongolia.

The glacier answered. Not in the condescending tone adults reserve for children, but in the egalitarian register of grief. It told me about the pressure of accumulation, how every decade another foot of snow compressed into an inch of ice, how the weight felt like a library of unsent love letters slowly being turned into diamonds. It told me about the day it first tasted rock flour, how the basalt tasted of copper pennies and regret. It told me about the moment it realized it was retreating, not advancing—that its own body was becoming a river it would never be able to drink. The confession took six minutes, maybe sixty; chronology loosens its tie at that latitude.

I began to cry, not prettily—no single cinematic tear—but in the sloppy, hiccupping way toddlers cry when they lose sight of their mother in a supermarket. The tears froze on my cheeks into tiny brittle lenses, each one reflecting the same bruised sky. I scraped them off with a mitten and tucked them into the breast pocket of my jacket, next to the foil blister pack of antidepressants I had sworn I would stop needing once I got far enough away from cell-phone towers. The glacier kept speaking, and I kept listening, until the only difference between its voice and mine was the tempo of melting.

Eventually the sun dragged a dirty thumbnail across the horizon, and the world remembered color. The crevasse walls blushed rose, then saffron, then the heartbreaking aquamarine of a 1956 Buick my grandfather used to drive me to Dairy Queen in, the one that smelled of cracked vinyl and pipe smoke. I stood up, knees creaking like bad fiction dialogue, and opened the notebook at last. Instead of prose, I drew a single vertical line top to bottom, pressed so hard it etched the next four pages. Underneath it I wrote: “This is the sound of time leaving.” Then I closed the book, walked down the moraine, and never once looked back at the glacier, because I knew it was still speaking inside me, a private broadcast that would require the rest of my life to finish receiving.

Years later, in a city where the louthing drones of the world’s richest man’s delivery vans replaces and ice fracture, I will wake at 3:07 a.m. to the same wet exhale outside my window. I will get out of bed, barefoot on fake-wood laminate, and press my palms against the wall that separates me from my neighbor who rehearses saxophone scales at dawn. The drywall will be warm from the day’s trapped heat, and for a moment I will swear I feel the faint tremor of a thousand miles of ice remembering its own name. I will open my mouth, produce the same Geiger counter click, and wait. Sometimes, if the traffic lights synchronize just right, the wall will answer: not with words, but with a tiny shift in temperature, a confession that pressure still exists, that love letters are still being compressed into diamonds, that libraries are still migrating downhill one grain at a time. I will stand there until my feet go numb, a private pilgrimage that requires no passport, only the willingness to listen to a grief old enough to vote.

And when the saxophone finally starts up—B-flat major scale, ascending—I will imagine it is the glacier learning to play jazz, bending notes the way it once bent geological time, making beauty out of the necessity of departure.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
